bio: Noah Berrie is a London-based sound artist, composer, and classically trained violinist working across installation, video, and performance. His practice explores the interplay between sound, space, and perception, often blurring the boundaries of comprehension and auditory experience.

In collaboration with Angelica Jopling, Berrie created Not Here, a sound piece of intertwining, circular narratives emanating from found objects dispersed in space. Inspired by walks through an empty city during COVID, the work reflects on human absence, with disembodied voices in conversation. As the listener navigates the space, meaning gradually dissolves, questioning how sound reverberates in ways beyond perception.

Berrie holds an MA in Music, Science and Technology from the Center for Computer Research in Music and Acoustics (CCRMA) at Stanford University, as well as a BA in Music Composition and a BS in Symbolic Systems. He was an artist-in-residence at the Sarabande Foundation (2023–24), and has undertaken residencies at the Audium Theatre of Sound, Tracey Emin Foundation, High House, and Studio Richter Mahr.

His work has been presented internationally at venues including Haus für Medienkunst, Gray Area, and Audium Theatre of Sound, among others. Recent projects include ANTIPHON (solo exhibition, 2026, Haus für Medienkunst), As Long as a Piece of String (Sarabande High Road), and Organ Music at Audium, San Francisco.

artists.name: Oleksandra Marston
bio: Oleksandra Martson is a figurative artist from Kyiv. Her recent exhibitions include the RA Summer Exhibition in London (2023), Kariatides at Dailininku Sajungos Galerija, Lithuania (2023), and Madonna’s Dream at Gallery RA, Ukraine (2023).

Martson combines painting and graphics in her art, exploring the human body as a symbol of our inner selves. Her signature style blends realistic painting with clean, dramatic compositions and graphic detail. She has been included in the Royal Academy Summer Exhibition in both 2023 and 2025, and has been longlisted for the Herbert Smith Freehills Portrait Award at the National Portrait Gallery in 2024, 2025, and 2026.

artists.name: Orfeo Tagiuri
bio: Orfeo Tagiuri lives and works in London. His multimedia practice spans painting, drawing, performance, film, woodcarving, animation, and music.

Tagiuri’s work intertwines emotion and memory, crafting a vivid montage of love’s fragile dance. Symbols of pain—piercing nails and dripping blood—lay bare the rawness of inner wounds, while delicate flowers and angelic wings evoke a sense of healing, transformation, and rebirth.

His recent exhibitions and presentations include The Stations of the Cross at St Giles Cripplegate Church, London (2023); Daybed at MACRO, Rome (2021); and Thoughts to Fall Asleep To at Sapling Gallery, London (2021). Earlier participations include Do Disturb at Palais de Tokyo, Paris (2018), Song I Wrote For My Four Year Old Nephew at Sans Titre, Paris (2018), and Fiorucci Art Trust’s Volcano Extravaganza in Stromboli (2016).

artists.name: Paul Barlow
bio: Paul Barlow (b. 1988, Sheffield, UK) lives and works in London and received his postgraduate diploma from the Royal Academy Schools (London) in 2021. He was an artist in residence at the Joseph and Anni Albers Foundation, Connecticut, in 2019.

His recent solo exhibitions include A bit at N/A Gallery, Seoul, South Korea (2023), Superfluids at Karsten Schubert Gallery, London (2022), and 00:00:00 at South Parade, London (2022). He also presented work in The RA Schools Degree Show at the Royal Academy of Arts, London, in 2021.

Recent group exhibitions include Timescapes at Shtager and Shch Gallery, London (2023), New Beginnings at Blindspot Gallery, Hong Kong (2022), and Tomorrow 2021 at White Cube (2021). He has also exhibited in Bloomberg New Contemporaries at South London Gallery (2021), 21 Days Lacuna at Slugtown & Arcade Gallery (2021), Show Off (Open Rehearsal) at Cooke Latham Gallery (2020), and Standstill at Kubik Gallery, Porto (2019).

artists.name: Pia Ortuno
bio: Pía Ortuño is a Costa Rican artist living and working in London. Her practice engages with materiality and tradition, drawing from classical techniques in marble and bronze to explore themes of transformation and permanence.

She graduated with a BA in Fine Arts from the University of Costa Rica (2019) before moving to Pietrasanta, Italy, where she studied ancient sculptural techniques and apprenticed under Jiménez Deredia in his Carrara studio. She later worked at the Fonderia Artistica Mariani (2020). In 2021, she co-founded Sala Salon, an artist-led space, and in 2022, she completed her MA in Painting at the Royal College of Art.

Her recent solo exhibitions include Down to the Roots at Cadogan Gallery, Milan (2025), The Day of His Wrath at Duarte Sequeira, Braga (2024), and A Blue Fire at Incubator Gallery, London (2022). Selected group exhibitions include Across the Pond at Eric Firestone Gallery, New York (2024), Matter at Flowers Gallery, London (2023), New Ancients at Guts Gallery, Milan (2023), and Books and Things at Helen J. Gallery, Los Angeles (2022).

artists.name: Seraphina Mutscheller
bio: Seraphina Mutscheller (b. Krefeld, Germany) is an artist living and working in London, UK. She is a recent graduate from the MA Painting Programme at the Royal College of Art, where she received the Windsor and Newton Award. Seraphina previously studied at the Slade school of Fine Art and City & Guilds of London Art School, graduating with the Chadwick Healey Painting Prize. Recent exhibitions include her MA Degree Show at the RCA and Polyphonies, Ames Yavuz Gallery, London (2025).

artists.name: Tamara Al-Mashouk
bio: Tamara Al-Mashouk is a visual artist whose multidisciplinary practice spans large-scale installations, performance, and video. Exploring the intersections of memory, the body, and architecture, her work reflects on movement, collective history, and place.

Rooted in Arabic traditions of hosting, Al-Mashouk’s practice responds to global crises such as Black Lives Matter, the Beirut explosion, and the occupation of Palestine. Through collaborations with transnational artists, poets, and performers, she creates spaces of solace, transforming grief and rage into love and action through art.
She has exhibited internationally at the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston; Yuan Museum, Beijing; Today Is Our Tomorrow festival, Helsinki; and Fábrica de Arte Cubano, Havana.

artists.name: Xavia Duke Richards
bio: Xavia Duke Richards (b. 1999, Jamaica) is a London-based artist whose practice explores notions of personal and societal freedom through richly textured, impasto painting. Raised in London, he is currently in his final year of study at Camberwell College of Arts.

Richards’ recent series employs thickly layered stirrups of paint, tarry dried pigment, and sand—potent materials that often explode beyond the confines of the canvas. His work engages with themes of resistance, movement, and constraint, using materiality as a means of challenging boundaries both physical and conceptual.

artists.name: Xiaochi Dong
bio: Born in 1993, Dong Xiaochi earned a master's degree from the Royal College of Art in the United Kingdom in 2023, and currently lives and works between London and Shanghai. He previously received an MA in Chinese Painting from the Shanghai Academy of Fine Arts in 2018, after completing undergraduate studies in the same department in 2015. His artistic practice is deeply rooted in classical Chinese painting, while drawing upon the experimental logic of contemporary art to form a language of subtle hybridity.

Dong takes inspiration from artificial landscapes of varying scales, including cultivated landscapes and garden systems, botanical conservatories, and miniature eco-systems. His works reflect a continuous fascination with nature and a quiet pursuit of care, simulation, and symbiosis. Drawing loosely on the notion of Gaianism, he explores the interconnectedness between organic life, built environments, and atmospheric cycles.

His creative endeavors primarily encompass painting and mixed-media pieces. By creating images saturated with hints of light, humidity and atmosphere, Dong aims to give form to contemporary images of nature.

artists.name: Yuma Radne
bio: Yuma Radné (b. 2001, Ulaan-Ude, Mongolia) is a painter based in London. She studied at the Slade School of Fine Art in London, having previously been enrolled at the Academy of Fine Arts Vienna and the Shtiglitz Academy in St. Petersburg.

Radné has presented solo exhibitions at galleries and institutions including the National Museum of Art of the Republic of Buryatia (2018), Bloom Galerie, Saint-Tropez (2023), and Steve Turner, Los Angeles (2023).

Her work has been exhibited at Art Fair Bordeaux with Bloom Galerie (2023); Enter Art Fair, Copenhagen, and Vienna Contemporary with Steve Turner (both 2023); Untitled Miami with Steve Turner (2024); and Future Fair in New York with Newcube (2024).

She has also participated in group exhibitions internationally, including at Fiumano Clase, London (2023); STUDIO WEST, London (2023); Hilger Next Gallery, Vienna (2022); Ars Pro Dono Gallery, Berlin (2021); Zaluu Centre, Ulaan-Ude, Russia (2021); Exhibit Studio, Vienna (2021); Semperdepot Academy of Fine Arts, Vienna (2021); and Art Revolution Taipei, Taiwan (2020), among others.
